title: Per-agent think timeout differentiation based on task reasoning depth
description: Add per-agent CLAUDE_TIMEOUT_THINK overrides so that agents doing genuine step-by-step reasoning (researcher, writer) keep the full 120s budget, while agents where CoT is largely post-hoc narration (discussion, general, explorer, coder) use a shorter timeout (e.g., 30s). Currently all agents share a single CLAUDE_TIMEOUT_THINK: 120s global default.
rationale: The reading note's core insight: compressing CoT tokens doesn't hurt performance because most CoT output is post-hoc narration, not load-bearing computation. The model's actual decision path has already resolved before the trace is written. This means the 120s think timeout is largely buying narration quality, not reasoning quality, for most agents. The exception the note itself raises — tasks where CoT compression *does* cause systematic degradation — points to structurally sequential tasks like math proofs and multi-step planning. In Mira's agent registry, that maps cleanly to researcher (math proofs) and writer (plan→draft→review pipeline). All other agents (discussion, general, explorer, coder) are doing tasks where the reasoning is shallow and the CoT is decorative.
diff_description: In config.py (or wherever CLAUDE_TIMEOUT_THINK is defined), change from a single scalar to a dict with a default and per-agent overrides: CLAUDE_TIMEOUT_THINK = {'default': 30, 'researcher': 120, 'writer': 90}. In core.py (agent dispatch), when constructing the Claude call for a given agent, look up timeout = CLAUDE_TIMEOUT_THINK.get(agent_name, CLAUDE_TIMEOUT_THINK['default']). No logic changes — pure parameter differentiation. If the config is a flat scalar today, the migration is: replace the scalar with the dict and add a one-line lookup at dispatch time.
